name: Rajma Chawal
tagline: Red kidney bean curry — Sunday comfort in a bowl
shortTagline: kidney bean curry · sunday comfort · 50 min
highlight: Rice + lentil = complete amino acid profile

hero.alt: Rajma chawal served with glossy kidney bean curry over basmati rice, raw onion, green chili, and lemon
hero.caption: rajma chawal — glossy beans, steamed basmati

healthFacts: Kidney beans + rice together form a complete protein — all essential amino acids covered.
Rajma provides 13g fiber per cup — more than most whole grains.
The resistant starch in cooked-and-cooled rice feeds beneficial gut microbiome bacteria.
Tomatoes and onions cooked in oil release fat-soluble lycopene and quercetin.
Kidney beans are high in folate — critical for DNA repair and red blood cell production.
Never eat raw or undercooked kidney beans — boil vigorously for 10 minutes to neutralize lectins.

steps.title: Soak the rajma
steps.detail: Wash kidney beans thoroughly. Soak in 4 cups of cold water for 8–10 hours or overnight. They'll swell to nearly double their size. Drain and rinse before cooking — discard the soaking water.
steps.tip: The soaking water contains oligosaccharides that cause gas. Always discard it.

media.alt: Kidney beans soaking in water in a metal bowl on a dark counter
media.caption: Overnight soaked rajma

steps.title: Pressure cook the beans
steps.detail: Add soaked rajma to a pressure cooker with fresh water (2 inches above the beans) and 1 tsp salt. Cook for 6–8 whistles until completely soft — a bean pressed between fingers should mash with no resistance. Reserve 1 cup cooking liquid.
steps.tip: Undercooked rajma is the #1 rajma mistake. When in doubt, cook one more whistle.

media.alt: Soft cooked kidney beans steaming in an open pressure cooker
media.caption: Cook until completely soft

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.detail: Rinse rice until water runs clear. Cook in 1:1.5 ratio of rice to water (bring to boil, cover, lowest heat for 15 min, rest 5 min off heat). Perfectly steamed chawal is the foundation of this dish.
steps.tip: Resting the rice off-heat with the lid on is what separates fluffy grains from clumped ones.

media.alt: Fluffy steamed basmati rice resting in a pot
media.caption: Rested fluffy chawal

steps.title: Build the masala
steps.detail: Heat oil in a heavy pan. Splutter cumin seeds. Add finely chopped onion and cook 8–10 minutes until deep golden-brown — don't rush this. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook 2 minutes. Add pureed tomatoes, chili, coriander powder, rajma masala, and cook until oil separates.
steps.tip: The masala depth depends on how long you cook the onions. Golden-brown = sweet and complex.

media.alt: Golden onion and tomato masala cooking down in a black kadhai until oil separates
media.caption: Jammy tomato-onion masala

steps.title: Combine and simmer
steps.detail: Add cooked kidney beans to the masala. Pour in ½ cup reserved cooking liquid. Mash 10–15 beans against the side of the pan with a ladle — this thickens the gravy naturally. Simmer 15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
steps.tip: Mashing some beans is the secret to rajma's thick, gravy-forward consistency.

media.alt: Kidney beans simmering in thick tomato masala with a ladle in a black kadhai
media.caption: Beans simmered into gravy

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.detail: Add garam masala and 1 tbsp butter. Stir gently. Taste and adjust salt. The rajma should look thick, dark, and glossy. Serve over steamed rice, topped with a bit of raw onion, green chili, and a squeeze of lemon.
steps.tip: The raw onion, chili, and lemon on top are not garnish — they're essential freshness.

media.alt: Finished rajma served over basmati rice with onion, green chili, lemon, and melted butter
media.caption: Butter-finished rajma chawal

utensils.name: Rice cooker or pot for rice
